text: The robotic arm pushes the cecum cranially while the surgeon detaches the mesenteric root using a bottom-to-up approach along the Garrotus fascia.
The surgeon uses bipolar forceps in the left hand and a monopolar hook in the right hand, manipulating tissues within the surgical field.
The surgeon continues cranial dissection until the third portion of the duodenum is visualized, using robotic instruments to carefully separate tissue layers.
The surgeon uses robotic arm I to push the transverse mesocolon cranially, while displacing the last ileal loop caudally, exposing the superior mesenteric axis.
The surgeon opens the visceral peritoneum, accessing the previously developed dissection plane using robotic instruments.
The surgical field shows a clear frontal view of the superior mesenteric vessels, facilitated by strategic port placement.
The surgeon uses bipolar forceps and a monopolar hook to dissect and section the ileocecal vein between clips, with the robotic arm assisting in maintaining exposure of the superior mesenteric axis.
The surgeon divides the ileocecal artery, which is located posteriorly to the superior mesenteric vein, using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon continues the dissection upwards along the superior mesenteric axis, using robotic arm I to maintain exposure of the area.
The surgeon divides the middle colic vein and the right branch of the middle colic artery at their roots, with visible separation of these vascular structures.
The surgeon performs a complete mesocolic excision, exposing the anterior aspect of the superior mesenteric vein, visible in the surgical field.
The final view reveals the vascular anatomy, showcasing the dissected and divided ileocecal artery and vein, along with the exposed superior mesenteric vein.
A sponge is placed underneath the transverse mesocolon to aid in identifying the correct surgical plane and to protect the underlying duodenum during the procedure.
The surgeon performs colonic mobilization and transects the greater omentum, separating it from the underlying structures.
The surgeon completes the division of the greater omentum, separating it from the underlying structures.
The surgeon assesses bowel perfusion using ICG-enhanced fluorescence, then divides the transverse colon with a 45mm blue cartridge robotic stapler.
The surgeon divides the terminal ileum, completing the separation of this section from the adjacent bowel.
The surgeon creates small enterotomies using a monopolar hook, visibly incising the intestinal tissue.
The surgeon performs a stapled side-to-side ileocolic anastomosis, joining the terminal ileum to the colon using surgical staples.
The surgeon closes the enterotomies using a double layer barbed Romney suture, securing the intestinal openings with precise stitching.
The specimen is extracted through a small incision created by connecting the two paramedian suprapubic trocars.
